“The Mississippi Blufflands are one of the ten last chance landscapes.”
— Scenic America

Our Goals

△

Create accessible spaces for public outdoor recreation

▲

Strive to preserve sensitive blufflands

▴

Restore and preserve natural habitats

Our History

La Crosse Public Library Archives

Conservation efforts for the La Crosse – La Crescent area blufflands began in 1909 when private citizens decided to preserve Grandad Bluff in the City of La Crosse.

By 1912, these conservationists had purchased the land from the La Crosse Stone Company and donated it to the City of La Crosse, to become Grandad Bluff and Hixon Forest.

When the first trails in Hixon Forest were built in 1975, it became clear that bluffland conservation efforts would be needed to help preserve the natural beauty of the area and maintain the trails. Today the Bluffland Coalition, working together with both public and private landowners, continues to preserve the area blufflands we know and love. As of 2022, approximately 5,000 acres of bluffland were preserved.

The Bluffland Coalition recognizes that the Coulee Region Blufflands occupy the ancestral homelands of the Ho-Chunk, Potawatomi, and Dakota people. We acknowledge these indigenous communities, and the sovereignty of all Native nations currently within Wisconsin, who have stewarded this land throughout the generations, and we honor their legacy of resistance and resilience.

3,000+Acres Protected

The Bluffland Traverse

54-Mile Point-to-Point Trail

Running the length of La Crosse County along the Mississippi River and its bluffs

A long-term goal of the Bluffland Coalition has been to create a trail system that connects existing trails in the blufflands to form one cohesive trail.

The Bluffland Traverse is a point-to-point route running from the northern end of La Crosse County to the southern end following the Mississippi River and the Bluffs that overlook it.

The route utilizes a combination of road, gravel bike path, shared-use trail, and single-track trail on public property to connect the county and its communities. The route currently runs about 54 miles.

The Bluffland Traverse Trail gives people greater access to the preserved bluffland properties in the area and allows for fantastic views of the bluffs, the Mississippi River, and the area we call home.

The best part about this trail is that it remains a continuous work in progress as the Bluffland Coalition continues to protect and preserve the blufflands.

Outdoor Recreation Alliance (ORA)

Preservation & Recreation

Mississippi Valley Conservancy
Mississippi Valley Conservancy

Our commitment to land preservation is shown through our stewardship and land management programs and efforts. Working directly with the Mississippi Valley Conservancy, Outdoor Recreation Alliance, and Friends of the Blufflands means that our properties are carefully managed to enhance natural biodiversity and protect the native ecosystems while providing exceptional trails for the public to enjoy.

Our properties and trails are a mixture of single and mixed-use trails that offer residents and visitors opportunities for recreation. Activities like hiking, trail running, mountain biking, and nature photography have become local favorites year-round.
